verses.commentary: Co-working with God creates responsibility not to receive grace in vain. Grace can be received fruitlessly if it produces no transformation. Response matters.
Paul quotes Isaiah 49:8 to declare now as the time of favor and salvation. The prophetic future has become present reality; urgency defines the current moment.
Avoiding stumbling blocks protects the ministry's credibility. Paul's ethical concern is not personal reputation but preventing discredit to the gospel itself.
Commendation comes through endurance in hardship. The triplet of troubles, hardships, and distresses introduces a catalog of apostolic suffering that authenticates ministry.
Beatings, imprisonments, riots, hard work, sleepless nights, and hunger form the résumé of apostolic legitimacy. Suffering credentials replace worldly credentials.
Character qualities and spiritual realities intermingle: purity, understanding, patience, kindness, Holy Spirit, sincere love. Inner virtues authenticate outer sufferings.
Truthful speech and God's power combine with weapons of righteousness for both hands. The imagery suggests comprehensive armament, offensive and defensive, rooted in righteousness.
Contradictory receptions: glory and dishonor, good report and bad report. Yet through all, genuineness persists despite being regarded as imposters. Reputation fluctuates; reality remains.
Known yet regarded as unknown, dying yet living, beaten yet not killed. The paradoxes reveal that apostolic existence defies worldly categorization.
Sorrowful yet always rejoicing, poor yet enriching many, having nothing yet possessing everything. The climax of paradoxes: ultimate poverty is ultimate wealth when measured by kingdom economics.
Paul speaks freely and opens his heart wide. The Corinthians ('Corinthians' by name) receive direct, unveiled affection. Transparency defines Paul's relationship with them.
The constraint is not on Paul's side but the Corinthians'. He has opened his affections fully; they are the ones withholding reciprocal love.
As a fair exchange, Paul appeals as to children: widen your hearts. The familial language intensifies the appeal for reciprocal affection.
Unequal yoking with unbelievers creates incompatibility. The metaphor from Deuteronomy 22:10 pictures two animals of different natures pulling the same plow, an impossible partnership.
Christ and Belial (worthlessness personified) share no harmony. Believer and unbeliever occupy incompatible spiritual spheres; partnership requires shared fundamental allegiance.
God's temple cannot agree with idols. Believers are the temple of the living God, echoing Leviticus 26:12 and Ezekiel 37:27. Divine indwelling precludes idolatry.
Separation is commanded. Quoting Isaiah 52:11, Paul calls for holy distinction from uncleanness. God receives those who come out and separate.
God becomes Father to those who separate. The promise echoes 2 Samuel 7:14 and Hosea, establishing family relationship as the reward for holy distinction.
